turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I keep getting a bunch of new allocations every loop iteration when I try to build text, but I thought Rust was supposed to be fast with memory.

That is a very common frustration for beginners. The issue is that you are likely using the standard concatenation operator or push_mut methods in a loop without planning ahead, which forces the String to grow and reallocate its internal buffer every time it gets full.
Oh, so I'm getting a lot of heap allocations instead of just one? That sounds terrible for performance.

Exactly. Every time the buffer fills up and needs to grow, Rust has to allocate more memory on the heap, copy the old data over, and then free the old block. If you are doing this in a loop, you end up with many small allocations instead of one big allocation, which is much slower.
Is there a specific pattern I should follow to avoid that? Like preallocating the capacity?

Yes, you should use String::with_capacity to estimate how many characters you expect beforehand. Then you can iterate through your data and call write! directly into that preallocated buffer without triggering any reallocations.
So I just need to make sure I know the size before starting?

Not necessarily, but it helps a lot. If you are unsure of the exact count, you can use write! with a default buffer and let String handle resizing internally, though preallocating is definitely better when you have an estimate to avoid repeated heap allocations in your loop.

So I'm building a list of user names into a big string, and it feels exactly like trying to bake cookies in a tiny oven that keeps getting broken every time I add another cookie. Every append seems to force me to rebuild the whole batch from scratch.

That's a classic frustration with mutable strings when you don't plan ahead. In Rust, the String type manages its own heap memory and resizing happens automatically when it gets full, which is why your approach feels inefficient. The solution is using String::with_capacity to reserve enough space upfront so you never need those costly reallocations.

Oh right, I remember hearing about capacity but I think everyone just guesses wrong anyway. Why can't we just keep appending without worrying? It seems like that's how most codebases handle this in practice.

You're seeing patterns from older languages or other crates where strings grow incrementally, which is fine for small data but terrible for large batches. In Rust specifically, if you don't call String::with_capacity before your loop, the runtime will keep allocating and copying memory blocks until you finish, which can be 10x slower than preallocating. The key insight is that write! lets you append safely without needing to clone the entire buffer every time.
Wait, so I should just use write! instead of push? But what if I make a mistake and overwrite something important?

Exactly right about your concern. The write! macro is designed specifically for this scenario because it writes into the String's internal buffer directly without creating temporary copies. If you accidentally write to the wrong position, you lose that data, but the safety comes from knowing exactly what you're targeting with the capacity hint.

I still feel like I'm going in circles here. Can't we just use a StringBuilder pattern or something similar? It seems like there has to be an easier way than manually managing all these capacities and buffers.

There is no magical builder crate that replaces this fundamental Rust concept because the performance cost of repeated allocations is real and unavoidable without preallocation. The write! macro combined with String::with_capacity gives you a direct path to avoid those costs, but it requires thinking ahead about your data size rather than relying on automatic growth. This is why experienced Rust developers always calculate their expected length before starting any string construction.
